data.title: WW International Is Great For Losing Weight And Losing Money For Shareholders
data.description:
Shareholders of WW International (WW) lost money as the company, the former Weight Watchers International. Inc, slid into bankruptcy last year and continued to see the new equity issued under the Ch.11 reorganization plan decline as the company restructured their business model. For a number of reasons that will be covered in this article, including too much expensive debt, WW stock remains a sell. This is an update to my prior WW articles.

Bankruptcy Reorganization Plan Was Irrational

WW International filed for Ch.11 bankruptcy, which I expected, on May 6, 2025 and exited bankruptcy on June 24. Under the reorganization plan (docket 18), WW shareholders were "gifted" a token recovery of 0.01073733 new WW share for each old WW share held. (Gifting often makes the bankruptcy process faster and less likely to have lawsuits filed by shareholders.) Based on the current WW price of $17.60, this recovery is currently worth about $0.19 per old (WGHTQ) share.

I think, however, that the reorganization plan was completely irrational because it resulted in WW being highly leveraged. First lien claim holders received 91% of the new stock and $465 million term loans with an interest rate of SOFR+6.80 for their $1.62 billion claim. The rate on this term loan was 10.49% as of 12/31/25.

Instead of having $465 million very expensive debt, WW could currently have no long-term debt if the first lien claim holders just received stock. The value of the stock received would, in theory, be worth more by not having the debt because the enterprise value remains the same. Not only are they now highly leveraged, they are paying almost $50 million annually for interest expenses. They could use this cash to expand their business instead of paying debt holders.

Over the last few years I constantly see irrational reorganization plans being confirmed by the courts which years ago "old school" bankruptcy judges would have rejected. For example, Spirit Airlines reorganization plan was irrational, which I covered, and they ended up back in bankruptcy court.

What Happened With Intangible Asset Values?

Their post-bankruptcy balance sheet using "fresh start accounting" is a complete shock. While goodwill declined, which was expected, from $239.593 million at the end of 2024 to $200.135 million at the end of 2025, net other intangible assets increased dramatically to $490.664 million at the end of 2025 from $115.762 million at the end of 2024.

Year-End Balance Sheet 2025 and 2024

This $374.902 million increase in other intangible assets is greater than the $318.071 million year-end 2025 total shareholder equity. Without this increase, shareholder equity would be a deficit of $56.831 million.

Determination of Net Other Intangible Assets 2025

Determination of Net Intangible Assets 2024

The primary reason for the increase was the $320 million for trade name. I am sorry, but I have serious doubts that their trade names are now worth $320 million. According to their 10-K this number is based on:

trade names valued under the relief from royalty income approach, the royalty rate was estimated to be 6.0%. For the developed technology-based intangibles that were valued using the relief from royalty income approach, the royalty rate was estimated to be 4.0% for Behavioral developed technology and 5.0% for Clinical developed technology

If future revenue does not significantly increase over an extended period of time, there could, in my opinion, be non-cash charges on future income statements to reflect a reduction in the carrying values of intangible assets.

Business Model Still Relies on Membership Subscriptions

WW International's business model still is based on membership subscriptions. This membership model, in my opinion, might have been appropriate when Oprah Winfrey was associated with Weight Watchers and there was a sense of belonging to a group for moral support, while trying to reduce weight via strict diets and exercise. Before they were not really selling a specific product - it was more of a service. Now they are selling specific products, including Wegovy which the FDA approved last December, and attempting to add on support services via their membership subscription program.

They are moving away from their traditional Weight Watchers behavioral subscription model, which they expect to drop 26% in 1Q '26 from 1Q '25, to clinical subscriptions, which they expect to increase to 200,000 at the end of 1Q '26. That is a sharp increase from 130,000 at the end of 2025. An aggressive and expensive marketing campaign in 1Q is a major factor behind this growth.

Membership is not cheap. According to their website:

Offer ends 3/31/26 at 11:59 PM PDT. Pay $25/month for your first two months with a 12-month WW Med+ plan purchase. After the first two months, membership continues for $74 per month for the remainder of your 12-month plan. Programs do not include the cost of GLP-1 medications. All medications require eligibility and a prescription. Plan auto-renews for another 12-month period at the same monthly rate at the end of each plan term unless canceled.

The $74/month does not include the actual cost for GLP-1. With recent soaring gasoline prices I can see why WW stock is dropping. Some people on tight budgets can't afford $74/month.

According to CEO Tara Comonte during the recent conference call "we're about so much more than just a prescription". OK, but is the "more" actually worth $74/month? This is what members get according to their website:

  • Meet with a Registered Dietitian for easier, tailored meal planning

  • Use your camera to track meals and their Points® value

  • 350+ ZeroPoint, no-track foods

  • Automatic macro tracking of any food you eat

  • Generate Points® value totals for any recipe on the internet

  • Access to more affordable weight-loss meds

Their potential growth is huge because according to the CDC 41.9% of adults over 20 are obese (obesity is defined as having a body mass index of over 30). I just think that many potential customers would just decide to buy GLP-1. drugs directly from a discount pharmacy and avoid paying $888 for year's membership. Taking GLP-1 is, in theory, the easy way to reduce weight. Why complicate this easy process with some expensive support subscription?

Valuation Based on Guidance Numbers

Management is expecting revenue of $620 - $635 million in 2026 with EBITDA of $105-$115 million.

This implies an enterprise value/EBITDA multiple of 4.18x - 4.58x. (Total long-term debt of $465 million minus cash of $160 million, plus equity capitalization of $176 million= $481 million enterprise value based on WW stock price of $17.60) These may seem like low multiples, but they are not, in my opinion.

First, you can not ignore "I" - interest expenses, which are almost $50 million annually. Second, the company is highly leveraged compared to the quality of balance sheet asset values. Third, since many consumers are being squeezed by higher gas prices, it might be difficult to achieve their EBITDA guidance numbers. Next, there is a high risk that their business model transformation will be less successful than WW bulls expect.

Conclusion

You would think that Weight Watchers should be booming since the number of obese adults has soared over the last few decades to almost 42% of the population. The problem is their membership subscription model - it is not cheap. I have serious doubts they can grow their clinical subscriptions to a level that will generate large profits.

WW International missed a chance in bankruptcy to become free of all long-term debt. Now they are highly leveraged with a weak balance sheet that has inflated intangible assets. At this point I still consider WW stock a sell.

data.title: Circle Falls As Stablecoin Future Questioned
data.description:
Tuesday was a very bad day for shareholders of Circle Internet Group, Inc. (CRCL). The company behind the USDC stablecoin saw its shares drop more than 20%, the worst trading day percentage wise since the company went public. The culprit seemingly was some potentially bad news on legislation regarding the Clarity Act, which could limit the future potential of the stablecoin space.

Previous coverage of the name

My most recent article on Circle came back in January, at which point I detailed a couple of major headwinds the company was facing. With U.S. interest rates dropping and USDC in circulation pulling back, it appeared that Circle's Q1 street revenue estimates were too high. Shares have rallied back more than 36% since, despite the S&P 500 (SP500) being down more than 5% over that time. However, Circle still remains well below its all-time high of just under $299 that was reached shortly after going public.

Recent legislative news

When President Trump was re-elected in 2024, the entire crypto industry celebrated as they believed he would be a major supporter of the space. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) surged to a new all-time high, stablecoins like USDC saw tremendous growth in circulation, and it seemed like nothing was impossible. Last year, a crypto winter started with prices falling, and the future of the industry suddenly didn't look as bright.

Earlier this week, some proposed new legislation regarding stablecoins seemingly put things into question for companies like Circle. The latest draft of the Clarity Act would ban stablecoin issuers from paying yield to customers just for holding the assets. Earning yields on balances, otherwise known as rewards, is an incentive for holding the coins, basically like getting interest on your deposits at a bank.

The latest part of the bill would potentially still allow for yields to be generated by using the stablecoin for payments, trading, or lending it out. In recent years, major banks have been opponents of stablecoin yields, as they are seen as a potential competitor. At a minimum, the latest news out of D.C. at least provides more uncertainty for the future of stablecoins, and it is not clear when and if final legislation will make it up for a vote.

An update on USDC growth

As a reminder, Circle generates a majority of its revenue from interest on the reserves that back the USDC stablecoin. 2025 was a huge year for USDC in circulation growth, surging by more than $31 billion. While U.S. interest rates declined, Circle was still able to report record revenue in Q4 that handily beat street estimates, rising 77% over the prior year period.

After topping out in mid-Q4 at around $78.5 billion, USDC in circulation took a hit as crypto prices fell. The number fell to around $70 billion earlier this year, but it has risen back towards new highs during March. Circle in most of its recent quarters had generated interest on its reserves that comes in almost 20 basis points below SOFR, which means in Q1 should be a net return of about 3.50% on an annualized basis.

While there is still some time left in the quarter, let's assume that USDC in circulation averaged about $75 billion for the quarter. That would mean about $656 million in interest revenue for Circle, and other revenues in the period could be around $40 million as management has guided to full year other revenues of $150 million to $170 million for the year. In the chart below, you can see how Street estimates for Q1 have trended over time.

Currently at $719 million, I still think the Street is a little too high for Q1 at the moment. There's still plenty of time for numbers to change, however, as we don't yet know how much USDC in circulation there will be at the end of the quarter. Perhaps the good news for Circle on the interest rate front is that expectations no longer call for multiple rate cuts from the Fed this year, which would eat further into the return rate that the company generates.

The current valuation picture

Circle trades at more than 91 times its expected adjusted earnings per share as of midday Wednesday. That's still well above the more than 48 times that Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) goes for. Both firms are expected to see their adjusted earnings decline this year, and a lot more so for Circle. However, Circle is expected to show more than 13% revenue growth this year, while Coinbase is projected for just 1.25%. On a price-to-sales basis, Circle has again flipped to being more expensive, going for 8.10 times this year's projected revenue, with Coinbase around 6.65 times.

When it comes to Street analysts, the picture is very bullish currently. Of the 23 analysts covering the stock, 9 have Strong Buy ratings, with 2 more having Buy ratings. There are 11 analysts with Hold ratings and 1 with a Strong Sell. The average price target is nearly $128, implying more than 22% upside from current levels. I should note, however, that the Street average has come down by $12 since my previous article, and it was $235 in the stock's early trading days, so analysts have reduced their valuations a bit over time.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Circle had its worst public trading day yet on Tuesday, as shares fell more than 20% due to potential legislative headwinds. There now seems to be an effort in Congress to limit stablecoin yields, potentially putting a major roadblock in front of the future of this emerging business. With the big banks trying to fight off this evolving financial technology, it remains to be seen what the final legislation will end up being.

Until we get a better idea of how this will all turn out, I am going to remain neutral on Circle shares. The latest news could turn out to be a major negative, but nothing is finalized just yet. On the flip side, USDC in circulation has rebounded from the early 2026 lows, and a lack of Fed rate cuts is good for the company's interest generation on reserves. I do think Q1 estimates still remain a little high, but at the moment the potential legislative roadblock could turn out to be a much larger issue than a single quarter's results.

data.description:
The focus of the portfolio is on balance sheet strength, profitability, earnings visibility and value.

Fund performance

  • Columbia Pyrford International Stock Fund Institutional Class shares (PISLX) returned 3.38% for the quarter ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index-Net, returned 4.86% for the same period.
  • Please check online at Financial Professionals | Columbia Threadneedle Investments US for monthly performance.

Fund strategy

A value- and quality-driven approach

Adopts a relatively defensive investment stance

Enhances absolute return potential

Maintains a focus on absolute returns, rather than relative risk, and diversifies across countries and sectors

Benefits from consistent process

Follows a disciplined long-term investment process executed by an experienced and highly-tenured investment team

Expense ratio

Shareclass No waiver (gross) With waiver (net)
Institutional 1.09% 0.90%
A 1.34% 1.15%

From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 12/31/2026, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Market overview

Global equity markets ended the year on a positive note, demonstrating resilience amid slowing but still positive economic growth, easing inflation pressures and continued geopolitical uncertainty. Investor sentiment improved, as major central banks shifted further toward a more accommodative policy stance, supporting equity valuations and contributing to lower market volatility. Developed markets outperformed emerging markets during the quarter, with U.S. equities benefiting from resilient corporate earnings, broadening market participation beyond mega cap technology and sustained investment linked to artificial intelligence (AI). European equities advanced on signs of economic stabilization and increased fiscal support. Performance across Asia and emerging markets was mixed, reflecting divergent policy paths, currency headwinds and uneven growth momentum. Overall, equity prices continued to climb, underpinned by resilient fundamentals and expectations of a measured monetary easing cycle into 2026.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future bond holdings are subject to risk.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Our relative underperformance is mainly explained by stock selection in the eurozone and in Norway.

In Europe, stock selection in the eurozone detracted. In the Netherlands, Wolters Kluwer underperformed as investors increasingly assessed the potential impact of AI on the information services sector. Attention was focused on its UpToDate clinical-decision-support platform, which represents a meaningful portion of group revenues, following the emergence of AI-based tools such as OpenEvidence that have gained rapid adoption in the U.S. While the competitive landscape is evolving, we believe Wolters Kluwer's long-term position is underpinned by the depth and reliability of its content, longstanding customer relationships and the integration of its products into hospital systems and clinical workflows. The implications of AI for the business model remain an area of investor focus and are likely to stay under scrutiny through 2026. In France, Air Liquide and Dassault Systemes underperformed. Air Liquide's high-profile Baytown blue ammonia project was frozen indefinitely, with Exxon flagging a lack of guaranteed buyers. This led to some concerns in the market that the energy transition, a key part of the Air Liquide growth story, could be delayed. Dassault Systemes detracted from performance following a late-period share-price decline, driven by softer sentiment toward software stocks and a moderation in near-term growth guidance — despite the company's strong competitive position, resilient industrial demand and improved long-term visibility from its ongoing cloud transition.

In Germany, shares in Deutsche Post jumped on its quarterly results, which beat consensus profit forecasts. This was primarily driven by better-than-expected performance in the Post and Parcel division. The Express division also saw profits grow, aided by effective cost control and despite a fall in volume due to pricing measures. The reiterated guidance for 2025 was taken positively by the market.

In Norway, stock selection detracted. Telenor's shares were weaker during the quarter, partly reflecting a consolidation after strong previous performance. The company's November capital markets day highlighted a sharper strategic focus on the Nordic region, and there is a growing expectation that Telenor might consider selling assets in Asia. In the Nordic region, Telenor is dependent on regulation. Sweden and Denmark represent two markets where Telenor could potentially consolidate to support a more rational competitive structure over time.

In Switzerland, stock selection added. Roche's outperformance was due to positive headline readouts on two of its important pipeline drugs. Expectations for trial success were low, and as a result consensus has been moving up on the news. There has been some clarity on U.S. drug pricing negotiations and tariffs, which has lifted the sector more generally.

In Australia, stock selection added. Rio Tinto performed strongly, delivering strong gains as commodity prices rose and investors rotated into diversified miners. Its new CEO's focus on portfolio simplification and cost control was well received at its investor day in December. Brambles detracted as its share price derated from earlier highs amid valuation normalization and broader market rotation, despite continued strong fundamentals and capital returns.

In Japan, stock selection added. Sumitomo Rubber contributed as earnings momentum improved into year end and upgraded guidance reinforced confidence in a cyclical recovery, underpinned by pricing discipline and strategic restructuring. Mitsubishi Electric delivered strong earnings that drove a share-price rally toward year-end, as investors reacted positively to improving outlooks in its Infrastructure and Life segments. ABC Mart detracted as its share price derated from earlier highs amid uneven monthly sales trends and margin pressure, despite resilient domestic demand supported by inbound tourism.

On a sector basis, our underweight to and stock selection within financials detracted. Stock selection in information technology and materials also detracted. Our stock selection in communication services and consumer staples added. Sector positioning is a residual of the opportunities we find at the country level.

Outlook

In 2026, investors will continue to grapple with geopolitical tensions, trade disruptions and potential inflation surprises linked to AI-related productivity and energy transitions. Companies with strong balance sheets, recurring revenues and durable competitive advantages remain well-positioned amid macro uncertainty. If inflation continues to trend lower, this will anchor bond yields and support risk assets.

The portfolio remains defensively positioned, with an underweight to the quantitative-easing-fueled markets and an overweight to the Southeast Asian markets. The European portfolio is concentrated in the U.K., Switzerland and the relatively healthy economies of core Europe. Economies in Southeast Asia offer sustainable economic growth, supported by increased labor output or productivity growth and trading at more reasonable valuations. We remain underweight to Japan, as its potential economic growth rate remains low, given its poor demographics and low productivity growth.

In terms of sectors, we have limited exposure to more-cyclical sectors such as financials and consumer discretionary. Overweight positions are held in defensive sectors such as industrials and communication services. These sectors offer predictable revenue streams and attractive valuations. The focus of the portfolio is on balance-sheet strength, profitability, earnings visibility and value.

As of period-end on December 31, 2025, the portfolio had no currency hedges in place.

data.title: Invesco Quality Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Yields shifted

Short and intermediate-maturity Treasury yields fell during the quarter. One- to five-year maturities fell between 0.01 and 0.20%, while longer maturities were relatively stable to slightly higher, resulting in steepening of the overall yield curve.

2 Inflation pressures persisted

Economic data, while limited by the US government shutdown, showed sticky inflation, with tariff-related cost pressures adding further uncertainty. These dynamics in our view increase the likelihood that the US Federal Reserve will keep monetary policy tight for an extended period.

3 Agency MBS benefited from yield curve steepening

US Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) had a positive return for the quarter. High coupon MBS outperformed as implied volatility declined and the yield curve steepened, which supported market segments delivering higher income.

Investment objective

The fund seeks to provide a high level of current income, with liquidity and safety of principal.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 509.90

Portfolio managers

Brian P. Norris, Clint W. Dudley, David Lyle

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Structured credit sectors outperformed Treasuries and investment-grade corporate bonds this quarter.
  • We favor a neutral allocation to Agency MBS because after strong performance in the third quarter, we believe valuations now appear fair relative to historical levels. Technical conditions (supply/demand balance) in our view remain supportive, but we believe the overall risk/reward profile is more balanced.

Portfolio characteristics*

30-day SEC unsubsidized yield (Class A shares) -

Portfolio positioning

We maintain a neutral allocation to Agency MBS, reflecting in our view fair valuations after strong recent performance.

Among 15-year mortgages, the fund is underweight in mortgages with 1.5% - 2.5% coupons and overweight in mortgages with 4.5% - 5.0% coupons.

data.title: ACWX: International Stocks May Benefit From Iran De-Escalation
data.description:
Introduction

Global equity markets have seen significant swings in recent weeks amid energy price volatility. While uncertainty is likely to remain high amid the ongoing war in Iran, a potential ceasefire may result in the outperformance of ETFs primarily invested in companies operating in net energy-importing countries.

Investors optimistic about a quick resolution to Middle East hostilities may want to consider the iShares MSCI ACWI ex US ETF (ACWX), which I rate with a Buy rating. Key sticking points behind my bullish outlook include:

  • An attractive 2.79% dividend yield achieved with a modest 0.54x payout ratio, notwithstanding the ETF's cyclical sector tilt.
  • A total return outlook of about 8.94%, with earnings growth driven by retained earnings and the ETF's small allocation to fast-growing emerging markets.
  • Significant diversification across individual holdings and countries, presenting less company-specific risks relative to the S&P 500.

Strategy and Key Characteristics

ACWX is a passive ETF investing in large- and mid-cap companies outside the United States (its benchmark is the MSCI ACWI ex-US index). ACWX invests in both developed and emerging markets, presenting a mix between institutional stability offered by developed markets (which account for the bulk of the ETF's investments), as well as higher GDP growth potential coming from emerging markets.

The portfolio is quite diversified, spanning some 1,727 individual companies, with allocation to the top ten holdings standing at about 14%. This is notably below the circa 37% weight the S&P 500 has in its top ten holdings, indicating that ACWI returns are much less dependent on individual company performance.

From a geographic perspective, allocation is primarily to energy-importing countries in Asia (Japan, China, and Taiwan) and Europe (United Kingdom, France, and Switzerland), although ACWX also has a circa 14% exposure to relative beneficiaries of the current energy crisis, such as Canada, Australia, and Brazil. As such, I believe that any potential de-escalation in Middle East hostilities will be a net benefit for ACWX.

The ETF's expense ratio stands at 0.32%, somewhat above the 0.1-0.2% I typically recommend for passive ETF holdings, but overall acceptable for buy-and-hold long-term investors.

Valuation and Sector Allocation

I calculate ACWX holdings currently trade at a trailing P/E of 17.4x (earnings yield of 5.75%). This remains notably more attractive relative to the S&P 500, where the trailing P/E stands at about 26.1x (earnings yield of 3.83%). As such, we see that current market pricing suggests that S&P 500 earnings would have to grow approximately 1.92% faster in perpetuity just to make up for higher starting valuations. I think this is somewhat unlikely, potentially indicating relative undervaluation of ACWX holdings.

Of course, expectations for near-term ACWX earnings growth are quite muted, as companies in energy-importing countries grapple with high fuel costs and a lack of supplies. Over the medium to long term, however, I am confident that economies will adapt to the new realities. What is more, a de-escalation in the Iran war may allow for a quick recovery.

Near-term headwinds aside, another key reason why ACWX holdings trade at lower valuations is that the ETF's allocation to cyclical sectors such as financials, industrials, consumer discretionary, materials, and energy is quite high, at around 60%. This makes ACWX earnings more volatile depending on the economic cycle, thus justifying a lower P/E multiple.

The ETF's allocation to the information technology sector is only 16.24%, roughly half that of the S&P 500, indicating smaller benefits from AI-related earnings growth for ACWX holdings.

To conclude, while war in the Middle East presents a near-term uncertainty that is reflected in lower valuations for ACWX holdings, I think that the ETF's current valuation provides an attractive entry point for long-term investors. In essence, my thinking is that as the war ends, or alternatively, companies adapt to the new reality, the valuation discount relative to the S&P 500 will begin to shrink.

Return Outlook and Rating

In light of attractive current valuations and a cyclical tilt, it is no surprise that ACWX offers a rather high dividend yield of 2.79%, with growth over the past decade standing at 6.66% per year. Factoring in the 0.32% expense ratio, I calculate that ACWX's payout ratio stands at 0.54x. As such, I think the ETF's distributions are relatively safe even if we see no quick de-escalation in the Iran war.

I arrive at the 0.54x payout ratio by using the 2.79% dividend yield, adding the 0.32% expense ratio to it, and dividing the sum by the earnings yield (5.75%) ACWX holdings trade at. In numbers, we get:

2.79%+0.32%=3.11% for the adjusted yield,

3.11%/5.75%=0.54x payout ratio

I think adding the expense ratio is pertinent as it shows ACWX's ability to pay out dividends after accounting for the expenses the ETF incurs. I opted to use the earnings yield in the calculation since it is updated on a daily basis on the iShares website, akin to the dividend yield. In essence, investors are buying a portfolio of stocks which has produced an earnings yield of 5.75% over the past twelve months, and this portfolio of stocks supports a 2.79% dividend distribution after expenses.

Turning to the benefit from retained earnings, I estimate ACWX holdings should see a circa 2.65% boost to earnings from reinvested profits alone. I view this estimate as quite conservative, since return on invested capital would typically be higher than the one implied in the earnings yield ACWX holdings trade at.

I arrive at the 2.65% retained earnings contribution by multiplying the retention ratio of 0.46x (1.00 minus the 0.54x payout ratio above) by the 5.75% earnings yield. In numbers, we get

0.46*5.75%=2.26% contribution from retained earnings

The third component driving ACWX returns will naturally be nominal economic growth in countries where ACWX holdings operate. While a prolonged war in the Middle East will likely result in a low-growth, high-inflation environment in the near term, over the long term I do expect ACWX holdings to benefit from nominal GDP growth of about 3.5%, as slower growth in Japan and Europe is somewhat offset by stronger GDP growth dynamics in emerging markets, Canada, and Australia. My calculations are summarized in the table below:

Return Component Contribution Commentary
Dividend 2.79% Relatively safe thanks to a low 0.54x payout ratio, notwithstanding high cyclical exposure.
Retained Earnings 2.65% Conservative estimate as return on invested capital likely above earnings yield.
Nominal GDP Growth 3.5% Too optimistic in the near term given the Middle East war, but overall achievable in the long term.
Total 8.94% Attractive relative to the S&P 500

Source: Author calculations

As you can tell from the table above, my calculations show a high single-digit total return outlook of about 8.94% for ACWX investors, which I see as quite attractive relative to the S&P 500. Hence I believe that a Buy rating is warranted for ACWX, notwithstanding risks, which I will discuss in the next section.

Risks

In the near term, a principal risk to consider is the ongoing war in the Middle East and its effect on cyclical company earnings, which account for roughly 60% of ACWX holdings. A prolonged conflict will likely result in a stagflationary environment, potentially resulting in ACWX undershooting my long-term return outlook of 8.94%.

While the allocation to emerging markets (circa 15% of ETF assets) such as China, India, and Brazil naturally results in a higher total return outlook thanks to their robust GDP growth dynamics, investors should note risks stemming from weaker corporate governance practices and a somewhat smaller link between emerging markets GDP growth and corporate earnings growth. As such, the anticipated 3.5% nominal earnings boost from economy-wide GDP growth may be somewhat optimistic.

A weaker U.S. dollar (DXY) was a tailwind for ACWX returns in 2025. That said, a reversal of dollar weakness may result in ACWX underperforming the S&P 500 in USD terms. This is due to the fact that the ETF employs no currency hedging, with its net asset value dependent on the prices of stocks traded around the world in various local currencies such as the EUR or JPY.

Conclusion

Despite war in the Middle East, the ACWX ETF has marginally outperformed the S&P 500 so far in 2026. This comes as international stocks remain notably cheaper relative to large-cap U.S. peers, providing a margin of safety for investors even as the earnings growth outlook is blurred in the near term.

I calculate ACWX should deliver a high single-digit total return over the medium to long term, with roughly a third of returns coming in the form of dividends. This should be enough for ACWX to outperform the S&P 500, warranting a Buy rating on the ETF.

Key risks in the investment case include ACWX's outsized allocation to cyclical sectors, which may backfire if the war in the Middle East drags on; the ETF's emerging markets exposure, as well as a potential reversal of recent U.S. dollar weakness.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Paychex Has Already Priced In Enough Risks, Entering May Pay Off
data.description:
It has only been two months since the publication of my previous coverage of Paychex, Inc. (PAYX). Despite its attractive valuation then, the stock price downtrend persisted and crossed below the $100 mark to its one-year low. I can’t blame the market due to the continued macroeconomic volatility and some uncertainty brought upon by AI. Yet, PAYX’s robust growth, intact fundamentals, and strategic concentration on SMEs position it well against these challenges. Technicals adhere to it as new buying opportunities emerge.

Q3 2026: Growth And Margins Are Attractive

In my previous coverage, I’ve already expressed my concerns about the external market forces that may affect the performance of Paychex, Inc. Macroeconomic volatility and RTO, or return-to-office, policies were additional concerns. But once again, PAYX proved that there’s no stopping yet as it sustains its growth with well-managed efficiency amid its expanding operating capacity. We have seen this in its most recent performance.

In Q3 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $1.81B, up by 19.8% YoY from $1.51B. This YoY growth was higher than in my previous coverage at 18.2%. In fact, this was its strongest YoY performance in the eight quarters, proving that its continued expansion has become more fruitful. As you can see, both its revenue components increased. The impact of its Paycor acquisition continued to materialize. But most importantly, PAYX maintained strong customer retention with over 800,000 clients.

Likewise, its total expenses rose by 24%, which completely offset revenue growth. But this wasn’t automatically a bad thing. After all, revenues were still high enough to cover the whole amount. We should also consider the impact of inflation on its cost pressures. More interestingly, its cost of service, which is mainly the direct cost of its operations, only increased by 11%. The huge bulk came from its SG&A expenses, which should not be surprising since it had more employees after the M&A. As a result, its operating margin decreased but remained high at 45.2%.

Challenges

In my previous coverage, I’ve already discussed the potential risk brought upon by AI. One is AI can cause job duplication, which might lead to mass layoffs across various industries. Lower employees mean fewer payroll seats catered to by HCM solutions. I think the woes concerning this aspect are still driving caution among investors.

Another factor is inflation because it remains stubborn, coupled with new tariffs. But an emerging challenge to most industries is the skyrocketing oil prices. Why? This will affect economies across the globe since this will lead to higher fuel prices. Fuel is a main component for the operations of shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial sectors. These are large and asset-heavy, which may impact many other industries, such as retail, consumer discretionary, and power. As a result, the general price level in the world may rise further. In turn, businesses may face higher cost pressures, affecting their budget per employee. Clients and/or payroll count under HCM solutions may decrease, especially if businesses shut down or lay off employees. PAYX may also experience higher expenses because it may also have to increase wages to help employees cope with elevated prices.

Mitigants

Despite these headwinds, I believe that PAYX may remain resilient.

One aspect is its high concentration on SMBs, or small and medium businesses. These are typically startups and/or those with limited budgets for innovation or AI. Investing in it may lead to high upfront costs, and most likely, this is not their priority. PAYX also provides PEO solutions to handle meticulous and complex HR tasks aside from payroll and employee benefits. These include tax filings and policy compliance. And even if SMBs decide to build their own HR teams, I believe that they will not just reduce their headcount because businesses like these are typically administrative and compliance driven. So, they still do not require headcount optimization. They will still need to shell out a lot of money and time to get advanced capabilities like automation and extensive data analytics. With that, PAYX may secure its client base despite AI woes today.

Another is that the business model of many HCM solution companies like PAYX is usually a combination of recurring and contractual. Businesses or clients may subscribe to its solutions paid monthly or every payroll. This helps PAYX maintain predictable cash flow and high visibility. Also, this business model helps it maintain strong client retention because it is not easy and cheap to switch to payroll providers whenever they want to. It will be time-consuming and costly to adopt new technologies and train employees to use them. It is not like a typical consumer good that consumers may disregard if the price goes up. The substitution effect in this industry is limited, which protects players even during an economic downturn.

Lastly, PAYX’s Balance Sheet is still robust despite the huge increase in borrowings after the M&A. Cash and cash equivalents of $1.7B are still high enough to cover the majority of current liabilities before client fund obligations. It is also more than enough to cover borrowings maturing within the year, accounts payable, and accrued expenses. More importantly, it maintains a decent debt maturity profile because 90% of the total will not mature within a year. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.1x, which means that PAYX only needs 1.1 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. This proves that it has enough buffer to sustain its increasing operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is still $4.32, so the dividend growth rate remains at 8.12%. However, the dividend yield is much more attractive at 4.77% because of the huge stock price drop. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 7.0% to be in line with my previous coverage. But note that the risk-free rate increased a bit to 4.3% in line with the 10-year treasury yield. Lastly, Beta is now 0.95, which means that PAYX is already less volatile than the S&P 500 and in my previous coverage. With all this information, I derived a TP, or target price, of $151.48.

I also made updates on price ratios and focused on its EPS and sales. Using P/E, the stock now trades at 20.50x, which is already cheaper than the five-year average of 30.86x. Also, the current multiple alone already shows that it is already fairly valued. Indeed, it is already trading at its cheapest level since 2021. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $136.38. The same applies to P/S, which suggests a TP of $152.99.

Technicals

PAYX’s technicals are still bearish as it stays at its one-year low. It moves below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing that the current momentum is weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day is still trending downwards, showing that market sentiments are still cautious.

Its MACD shows the same thing as the MACD line remains flat. On a lighter note, we can see some rebound attempts if you look at its increasing histogram, which implies that buying volume is starting to catch up again with selling volume. After all, its sell-off opened new buying opportunities for traders. Its RSI is still consistent with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence yet. Even so, its RSI within this range shows that there is wide buying room at this price.

Conclusion

Paychex, Inc. maintains its robust growth and margins despite the headwinds and AI skepticism that the HCM industry faces. This is supported by its stellar Balance Sheet and strategic business model. Its valuation is already cheap and shows upside potential. Technicals are still weak, but the continued selling trend opens new buying opportunities at a discount. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: EZA As A High-Beta Bet On Metals
data.description:
During some recent digging into country ETFs, I came across the iShares MSCI South Africa ETF (EZA). I found this interesting for several reasons, not least of which due to its intense concentration in miners and financials. On the plus side, this gives significant high-beta exposure to metals, which is compelling given the recent rallies. On the downside, it exposes investors to specific macro risks that are very important to keep in mind when evaluating the fund. Thanks to the attractive dividend yield, investors are being paid to wait, but they may have to wait a long time. I rate EZA a Hold.

What You’re Actually Buying

Let’s take a look under the hood of this country ETF. The first thing you’ll notice is the extreme top-heavy concentration. The fund’s top 10 holdings make up no less than 67% of the total ETF, which is an eye-watering amount by any standard. For comparison, the top 10 constituents of the VanEck Africa Index ETF (AFK), which is already top-heavy by country ETF standards, make up 40% of holdings.

Digging deeper into those holdings, we can see that the ETF basically exposes investors to two sectors: mining and banking. At more than 13%, AngloGold Ashanti plc (AU) is the fund’s biggest holding, followed by Gold Fields Limited (GFI) at around 11%. Overall, basic materials make up just over 32% of the ETF, with financials as a close second at 29%. The biggest financial holding is FirstRand Limited (FANDY) at around 6.5% of holdings.

The two sectors are distinct but do reinforce each other. The miners make money by selling gold and platinum group metals to the global market, earning in dollars. They spend this money mostly in rand on things like labor, energy, and logistics. This means that a weak rand boosts profitability. When metal prices rise, earnings expand very quickly due to operating leverage. The same mechanism means that earnings compress fast when metal prices drop.

Banks, on the other hand, act as a domestic macro amplifier. They make money through lending, payments, accounts, and advisory, and therefore do well when the commodity sector is strong and the rand is stable. What this means for the ETF is that commodity booms stack the upside, and downturns lead to deeper crashes. As such, the ETF behaves like a high-beta cyclical trade.

Explaining the Discount

The iShares MSCI South Africa ETF currently trades at a TTM P/E ratio of around 13.7x, according to Yahoo Finance. That’s very cheap, and we should unpack why the country’s market as a whole is trading at a discount.

First and foremost, the geopolitical risks. The African National Congress has ruled the country since 1994, but electoral support has declined significantly in recent election cycles in favor of opposition parties such as the Democratic Alliance and Economic Freedom Fighters. As such, there has been a coalition government since 2024, and concerns regarding fragmentation and policy uncertainty are widening sovereign bond spreads. Structural factors such as chronic electricity shortages, high unemployment, weak GDP forecasts, and rising public debt mean investors are paying a risk premium to participate in the market.

Secondly, the metals markets are highly cyclical. This means that multiples can quickly expand during commodity upcycles but compress just as rapidly during downcycles. Market reratings are episodic rather than structural and sustained. Therefore, low-teens earnings multiples reflect priced-in macro and cycle risk, as well as uncertainty regarding future earnings growth. At the moment, miners are doing well thanks to high metals prices. When this rally fades, investors may be left holding the bag. The healthy 5% dividend yield provides some cushioning for this, but even dividend payments are uncertain if macro factors turn against the play. Foreign investment plays a crucial role in South African equity performance, as periods of improving global risk sentiment and tighter sovereign bond spreads can drive sharp inflows and multiple expansion, while risk-off episodes often trigger capital outflows, currency weakness, and rapid valuation compression. Investors should view EZA as a timing-sensitive deep value position rather than a buy-and-hold compounder.

Risks and Catalysts

The main risks to the Hold thesis are weakness in the rand, a reversal in commodity prices (gold and platinum in particular), continued weak domestic growth, and political uncertainty. The ETF is extremely concentrated, which means it will bear the full brunt of any of these issues. Rising bond yields would improve lending earnings for banks, but at the same time would increase costs for miners looking to expand. A weak rand combined with falling commodity prices would make this a Sell. This risk is further amplified by the fact that timing commodity cycles is extremely difficult, and even if miner execution is perfect, the narrative falls apart if commodities don’t support the play.

On the upside, continued strength in gold and platinum would drive rapid earnings upgrades and dividend growth among the major players like AngloGold Ashanti and would also strengthen the banking sector. Combined with improved electricity reliability, risk-on sentiment for emerging markets as a whole, and stabilization in sovereign bond yields, this ETF could really deliver significant upside as forward earnings multiples suddenly look both cheap and attractive.

Conclusion

The iShares MSCI South Africa ETF is a high-beta play on metals. Timing commodity cycles is difficult at the best of times, but when you factor in geopolitical uncertainty, currency volatility, and weak infrastructure, it becomes a real challenge. Because the ETF is so concentrated in metals and financials, there is little room for error. While the dividend offers some downside protection, it is not nearly enough if the current commodity cycle turns. As such, the structural discount is justified, and reratings are episodic. Until upside catalysts become clearer, there is little reason to risk getting caught out.

data.title: Blaize: Revenue Guidance Is Vindicated, There's Light At The End Of The Tunnel
data.description:
Back in May 2025, I updated my coverage for Blaize Holdings, Inc. (BZAI), arguing the company would need at least another year for a bullish, high-stakes thesis to play out.

One year has not yet fully passed. Yet, after reviewing Q4 earnings, I believe we are seeing some light at the end of the tunnel. After a gruesome 2025 for shareholders (with the stock losing more than 90% since IPO), the business is finally accelerating, proving management’s initial IPO guidance is actually within reach.

Q4 earnings: management’s guidance was proven correct!

The main risk with a company like Blaize Holdings lies in management’s guidance. Since the company is new to the market, there was effectively no way to know whether guidance could be trusted when they IPO’d. That is especially relevant in the context of SPACs, which are often criticized for failing to create value to (new) shareholders.

With Q4 earnings out, we now know that Blaize’s management can, in fact, be trusted. The company reached more than mid-point of their own 2025 guidance, and they beat the upper end of guidance for Q4.

Expenses were however higher than initial guidance, and the company lost more than $100 Mln from operations, and double that when accounting for change in fair value of Legacy Blaize convertible notes and warrants.

The table below summarizes how actuals compare with 2025 guidance, as well as the changes in 2026 guidance as of these last earnings.

Data in Millions
